data.title: Gold And Silver Bull Market Has Much More Room To Run
data.description:
“It doesn’t do anything but sit there and look at you.”

— Warren Buffett on gold

Legendary billionaire investor Warren Buffett is well known for favoring investing in productive and profitable businesses, rather than commodities like gold.

Most investors agree with Buffett. Indeed, only about 10% of Americans own gold today.

It is true that stocks have significantly outperformed gold over long periods. However, gold doesn’t just “sit there and look at you”. As with any investment traded on financial markets, gold can generate strong returns. In fact, gold can outperform stocks for a decade or more. I believe it is not too late for investors to understand the drivers of gold prices and how to profit from the current gold (and silver) bull market.

Gold Prices Over The Past Century

Under the gold standard before 1934, gold was fixed to the US dollar at $20.67 per ounce. Then, under the Gold Reserve Act of 1934, the dollar was devalued and gold was revalued by 70% to $35 per ounce. Incredibly, this act also prohibited American citizens from owning gold and forced them to sell their gold to the US government at $35 per ounce.

Then in 1971, President Nixon threw in the towel after decades of Federal Reserve money creation and severed all ties between the dollar and gold. This allowed gold prices to float freely on the market. It also enabled the Federal Reserve to create money out of thin air at a much more aggressive pace than in the past, resulting in the “stagflationary” 1970s and culminating with the Fed increasing the supply of dollars by 40% in 2020 in reaction to the covid panic. As a result, the US dollar has lost about 87% of its purchasing power since 1971!

As a side note, after four decades, Americans were finally allowed to own gold again in 1974. This enables us to legally invest in gold to profit from its bull markets.

Stocks Versus Gold Cycles

The chart below shows the price of the S&P 500 stock index (not including dividends) relative to gold since the early 1970s. As this chart shows, the S&P 500 underperformed gold for nine years during the 1970s. Then the S&P 500 outperformed gold for 20 years during the huge stock bull market of the 1980s and 1990s. Then, the S&P 500 underperformed gold for 11 years until 2011. The S&P 500 has outperformed gold over the past 13 years, although they have performed similarly over the past six years.

What Causes Stocks Versus Gold Cycles?

While there are a wide variety of fundamental and technical factors impacting stock and gold prices, I believe the key driver of the waxing and waning cycles of stocks versus gold is valuation differentials.

The following chart shows Warren Buffett’s preferred valuation metric for the stock market: total stock market capitalization to GDP ratio. I placed green circles on two periods where stock market valuations were very low and stocks began a long bull cycle versus gold: the early 1980s and after the Great Recession of 2008-2009.

I also placed red circles on three periods where stock valuations were very high based on this metric: the late 1960s, 2000 and now. Since the bear market cycles of stocks relative to gold started near the valuation peaks in the late 1960s and 2000, we should not be surprised if another stocks versus gold bear market cycle starts at some point in the not-too-distant future, since stock market valuations are currently at the highest levels in history.

Gold And Silver Bull And Bear Markets

As with any financial market investment, gold and silver have their own bull and bear market cycles. The chart below shows that gold has had three bull market and two bear market cycles since the US severed all ties to gold in the early 1970s. The bull market of the 1970s resulted in a massive 1,480% return, likely aided by the fact that gold was catching up to decades of money creation and inflation. The bull market of the 2000s drove a strong 610% return. The current bull market that started in 2015 has resulted in a 150% return so far. If it were to eventually match the 610% return of the 2000s bull market, the price of gold would rise to about $7,500, which would provide a 175% return from current prices.

This next chart shows that silver’s bull and bear markets have been even more dramatic than gold’s. The 1970s silver bull market drove a huge 2,460% return and the 2000s bull market drove a 790% return, much higher than the gold bull market returns. So far, silver is up 140% in the current bull market. If this silver bull market were to be as strong as the 2000s bull market, silver would reach about $120. That is more than triple the current price.

How Is This Bull Market Different Than The Previous Bull Market?

In addition to valuation differentials, gold and silver bull markets are also driven by increasing economic, financial, monetary and geopolitical uncertainty. Since gold and silver served as money throughout most of the human civilization until 1971, during times of increasing uncertainty and risk, people seek out the safety of “real money”: gold and silver.

During the previous gold bull market from 1999 to 2011 (note: the silver bull market started in 2001, after the early 2000s recession ended), there were two recessions and stock bear markets, as well as the 9/11 terrorist attacks and various “wars on terror”, as well as unprecedented money creation by the Federal Reserve, including their new “tool” called “quantitative easing“.

Since gold and silver bottomed in 2015, we have seen the first-ever government “lockdowns” relating to the covid pandemic, then an unprecedented 40% increase in the money supply by the Fed which led to the highest inflation rates in four decades, a bear market in 2022 (and the median stock, as represented by the Value Line Geometric Index, is still 10% lower than in late 2021), one of the most aggressive Fed rate hiking cycles in history, the longest yield curve inversion since the Great Depression, significant political division in the US and wars with Russia and the Middle East.

Given the Fed rate hikes and yield curve inversion, it would be unprecedented to not have a recession and stock bear market, during which the Fed may conduct even more aggressive money creation than they have in the past. And these wars could broaden and become much more dangerous for the world. Thus, the recipe for a precious metals bull market similar to or even bigger than the 2000s bull market is in place.

One aspect of this recipe that supports the case for an even bigger precious metals bull market is the ratio of gold prices to the Fed’s monetary base, which is currency plus bank reserves. As shown below, this ratio was lower at the 2015 low (and is still lower now) than it was at the gold price lows in the early 1970s and 2000 (green circles). With the Fed likely to increase the monetary base significantly during its coming easing cycle, the price of gold has a long way to go to catch up to all of this money creation.

Gold and Silver Prices In A Recession

The previous long-term gold chart showed that gold was flat to up during five of the seven recessions since Nixon severed all ties to the dollar. It was only during the disinflationary or deflationary recessions of 1982 and 2008-2009 that gold declined materially, although those declines were relatively short-lived and gold generally recovered its losses by the time those recessions ended.

This chart shows that gold outperformed silver during six of the last seven recessions (shaded gray), which makes sense, as silver is more of an industrial metal than gold and is more sensitive to the boom-bust business cycle.

Silver and Gold Miners Follow Gold

This next chart shows that gold (black line) and silver (red line) generally track each other closely over time, although they are not perfectly in sync at all times.

This chart shows that gold (black line) and the Philadelphia Gold and Silver Index of precious metal mining stocks (red line) also generally track each other over time. However, due to their high operating and financial leverage, mining stocks typically outperform gold during gold bull markets and underperform during gold bear markets.

Gold, Silver And Mining Stock Risks

As with any investment, gold, silver and mining stocks have risks. As I have shown, gold and silver prices can decline significantly for long periods of time, even with lots of inflation and Fed money creation. That is why I recommend focusing closely on key technical indicators to identify when a bull market is turning into a bear market, as I discussed in this Seeking Alpha article.

The primary fundamental risks to gold and silver prices include 1) a recession, although that is more of a risk for silver than gold and more of a risk for stocks than either silver or gold, 2) significant disinflation or deflation, 3) less aggressive Fed monetary policy and 4) increasing world peace, prosperity and certainty.

Note that gold and silver ETFs are taxed as "collectibles", so they have a higher long-term capital gains tax rate of 28% compared to the maximum 20% rate for stock ETFs. Thus, holding gold and silver ETFs in a tax-free account like an IRA is better than in a taxable account.

Individual gold and silver mining companies have additional operational and financial risks than just the price of gold and silver, including leverage risks, regulatory and legal risks, environmental and exploration risks, currency risks, management execution risks, etc. This is similar to the operational and financial risks facing any individual company. That is why if one is investing in mining stocks, it is generally wise to diversify with ETFs, as I discuss below.

Investment Summary

Given the all-time high valuation level of the stock market, as well as numerous factors causing greater uncertainty for investors, I think gold and silver, as well as gold and silver mining stocks, are likely to offer significant upside potential for investors in the years to come. All that glitters is not gold, but this precious metals bull market promises to be very profitable for wise investors.

There are many ways to invest in gold and silver, including with physical coins, bullion and individual gold and silver mining stocks, but the easiest way to get started is with popular ETFs like SPDR® Gold Shares ETF (NYSEARCA:GLD) with a Seeking Alpha Quant Rating of 4.2, iShares Silver Trust ETF (NYSEARCA:SLV) with a Seeking Alpha Quant Rating of 4.4, VanEck Gold Miners ETF (NYSEARCA:GDX) with a Seeking Alpha Quant Rating of 4.42 and Global X Silver Miners ETF (NYSEARCA:SIL) with a Seeking Alpha Quant Rating of 4.44.

data.title: Main Street Vs. Blackstone Secured Lending: One Is Superior Pick For Defense
data.description:
Blackstone Secured Lending (NYSE:BXSL) and Main Street Capital (NYSE:MAIN) are arguably one of the most defensive investment choices in the entire BDC sector. Both BDCs fall into the Top 10 list of the largest players in the sector, and as of now they also both carry premium over NAV, which is not that surprising given the strength of the underlying fundamentals.

In my opinion, it would not be wrong to assume that most prudent BDC investors have included BXSL and MAIN in their portfolios, together with other large-cap and high-quality names such as Hercules Capital (HTGC) and Ares Capital (ARCC).

If we look at the total return performance chart below, we will clearly see how over the past 3-year period, BXSL and MAIN have delivered somewhat correlated returns, beating the index (BIZD) in a meaningful fashion.

In fact, if I was forced to cherry-pick only two BDCs for an inclusion in a defensive bucket of a BDC investment portfolio, I would certainly go with BXSL and MAIN.

Having said that, while both of these BDCs share similar defensive characteristics, at specific fundamental level, there are meaningful differences.

Let's now compare them side by side to determine which one of them could be a better fit for durable income seeking BDC investors. Yet, by saying this, I want to once again underscore that even though the analysis below will lead to a more favorable conclusion towards one of them, they both are inherently attractive and, in my opinion, should be seriously considered as core allocations in any BDC holdings list (unless the objective is to access high risk and high reward profiles).

Valuations and yield

As mentioned earlier in the article, both BDCs trade at a premium over NAV. The chart here depicts the historical price to book value (close reflection of P/NAV metric) dynamics for MAIN and BXSL.

From this it is very clear that MAIN is much more expensive than BXSL. The only BDC, which trades at a higher premium is HTGC (i.e., also a defensive BDC with focus on late stage, de-risked VC businesses).

As a result of the premium, the dividend yield for MAIN stands at 7.8%, which is one of the lowest in the entire BDC sector. Excluding supplemental dividends, MAIN's current yield drops even further to 5.7%. BXSL, however, offers 9.9% in dividend, which is fully comprised of base distributions.

Having a notable premium over NAV could indeed be deemed as a negative, introducing a drag on the potential upside or additional risk on the downside from potentially more magnified multiple compression.

Yet, there are two important aspects that I would like to stress in this context:

  1. MAIN has been always trading at a significant premium over NAV (the only short exception was right in the moment when the COVID-19 broke out and the economy was shut down).
  2. MAIN can use its shares as a currency to fund sizeable portfolio growth without impairing its balance sheet.

Portfolio and earnings structure

On the portfolio investment allocation level, MAIN and BXSL are different.

The case for BXSL is simple as it could be considered a pure play senior first lien secured focused BDC, investing in relatively large cap companies with annual EBITDA generation around $200 million. Currently, 98.6% of the total exposure lies in this investment category, which is the safest what one can achieve in the BDC segment. Hence, if we look at the earnings quality, we will see that the lion's share (93%) stems from cash income.

For MAIN the portfolio is different. The total portfolio (measured at fair value) is comprised of 51% LMM investments, 37% private loans, 4% middle market investments, and 8% is spread across multiple unrelated exposures. Over 99% of debt investments are also located in the first lien structures. Since MAIN usually provides meaningful equity financing together with debt capital to the underlying portfolio companies, there is a more material chunk of earnings generation that is associated with theoretically higher volatility income sources (e.g., dividend income, fees and proceeds from equity monetization).

Now, each of the two portfolio structures offer their own distinct advantages. For BXSL the focus on larger businesses with no meaningful exposure towards equity investments introduce a decent layer of safety as the small-cap risk is largely taken out of the equation. For MAIN, however, the fact that the portfolio is spread across different forms of assets enable stronger diversification and, more importantly, enhanced optionality on new deal origination front.

However, looking at the underlying credit statistics, we will have to conclude that MAIN has a higher quality portfolio.

For example, the average LTV of BXSL's investment positions stands at 47.5%, which is well-below the sector average of around 55%. Plus, the portfolio company weighted average EBITDA has been quite consistently rising, registering ~ 10% year-over-year growth based on Q2, 2024 period. The portfolio company average EBITDA margin is also higher than in the BDC sector - 27% vs. 23%.

Looking at MAIN, the statistics are even stronger. The weighted average senior leverage of MAIN's investments are 2.8x EBITDA, and senior interest coverage to EBITDA stands at 2.4x, which is, arguably, one of the best levels (metrics) in the sector. Plus, 64% of LMM portfolio investment companies pay dividends, which is another indicative of a healthy portfolio.

Risk mitigation

From the risk mitigation perspective (especially with a focus on dividend sustainability), there are couple of critical aspects, which are worth considering to determine which BDC has a higher margin of safety.

First, as of Q2, 2024, MAIN had an external leverage of 88%, whereas for the BXSL the relevant metric stood at 112%. While both leverage levels are below sector average of 116% and could be viewed positively, it is clear that MAIN has a healthier capital structure. In fact, I would argue that MAIN has slightly overcapitalized balance sheet, which, at some point, when the M&A markets start to become more active, could be put at work (together with attractive equity capital) to fund portfolio growth without assuming too much of a financial risk.

Second, as opposed to BXSL, MAIN has a decent exposure towards equity investments, which offer an important layer of defense. We could view MAIN's equity investments as an embedded liquidity, which during a positive M&A environment could produce huge amounts of additional cash inflows in an uncorrelated manner to the underlying cash interest income from debt-related assets. To put things into perspective, as of Q2, 2024, MAIN had about $6.00 per share in cumulative, pre-tax net unrealized appreciation, which, if fully realized, could cover the current dividend for exactly two years in a row. Granted, it is practically almost impossible to access all of the embedded value in a single quarter, but given the size of this amount, even a minor monetization activity could lead to a tangible impact.

Third, MAIN has never decreased its monthly base dividend in its entire ~17 years history, which includes the GFC and COVID-19. BXSL has been public only since 2021, so there is not that long track record for us to assess. Currently, the base dividend coverage for MAIN is at 149%, which is the second highest level in the sector. Factoring in the supplemental distributions, the total dividend coverage lands at ~ 109%. The total dividend coverage for BXSL is 116%, which is slightly better, but we have to remember that there is no meaningful equity value source that periodically sends the dividend coverage ratio materially higher.

The bottom line

In this article, I have compared my two favorite BDCs, when it comes to defense and yield predictability. While I remain bullish on both BXSL and MAIN, if I had to choose only one of them, I would go with MAIN.

In my opinion, as the analysis showed above, MAIN has simply a stronger margin of safety embedded in its portfolio and business model that, ultimately, enhances the sustainability of the current dividend.

The key drawback (apart from smaller yield) in MAIN's case is the notable premium over NAV, which theoretically could impose headwinds for further upside capture or in times of distress magnify the downside through multiple compression.

Yet again, in terms of maximizing protection for the current yield, MAIN is a better choice.

data.title: PBDC: Right BDC Income Play
data.description:
Introduction

BDCs are often best known for the accesses to the lucrative Private credit market with $1.7 trillion in assets. BDCs are always important assets for investors seeking for double digit income. Due to concerns about declining yields and rising default rates, the BDC market has been under pressure in recent months. With the rates expected to come down further, is now a time to get into BDCs?

One interesting approach is to start with BDC fund such as Putnam BDC Income ETF(NYSEARCA:PBDC), which has over 20 BDCs in its portfolio. The ETF pays monthly dividend with close to 10% yield, which should be very attractive income from a portfolio of credits. This actively managed BDC ETF has shown strong performance since it inception in about two years ago. I think PBDC is a good buy for income investors who feel that it is the time to get BDC exposures under the current macro backdrop.

The PBDC ETF Fund

PBDC is an actively managed ETF by Putnam. It is one of very few ETFs designed to track BDCs. Its official description is pretty straightforward as shown below:

The fund seeks current income. The fund invests mainly in BDCs (business development companies) that contribute to stability of dividend income and return potential.

Note that the "stability" mentioned above is the important part of the fund objective, on top of the high yield income. This is largely because of a portfolio of 22 BDCs that are being actively managed. The portfolio itself offers good diversification of the credit exposures, as well as the different BDC assets.

The following shows top ten BDC holdings in PBDC portfolio. As it can be seen that the first 4 constituents account for about 42% of the entire portfolio. So it is a top heavy ETF.

On the other hand, the ETF has been focusing on the performance of the returns. The top 5 have shown much better 1-Year returns than the rest of top 10. As shown in the second last column above, the top half has higher 1-Year return by about 10 percentage points in average. Keep in mind that top 10 represent over 75% of the portfolio and these are some of the largest BDCs in the market.

The following is a summary of the key market characteristics for PBDC

  • AUM: $108.53M. It is still a small ETF.
  • Volume(latest): 45,885
  • Inception: September 29, 2022.
  • Yield: 9.68%.
  • Expense Ratio:13.94%. The expense of BDC holdings are reflected.
  • Risk Score(Morningstar):77. It is considered "aggressive", due to the small-sized companies of the credits.
  • Volatility(52-Week HV): 12.2. It is comparable to S&P 500 index.

Turnaround may be near for BDCs

In 2024, with the decrease in interest rates, it is estimated that BDCs are down by about 2% so far this year. I believe that the following is the list of challenges the sector has been facing:

  • Known as "idiosyncratic losses", the credit losses of borrowing companies are higher, as these companies have been struggling to pay the interest charges set during the rapid hiking cycle with interest rates rising by 550 basis points in the past two years.
  • The risks involved are increasing: the default rate of private credit has risen to 5%. In fact, some private debt funds are rated only one notch above the junk status.
  • On the corporate side, companies are attempting to exit expensive private transactions or transfer assets from direct lenders.
  • The slow growth of the US economy has led to a declined ROEs across the sector group. The increased competition has driven down NII margins due to the lower spreads on loans.

However, I believe there is no evidence to suggest truly observed systemic losses across the board. In fact some of the default rate remains on a down trajectory as reported:

S&P Global Ratings Credit Research & Insights expects the U.S. trailing-12-month speculative-grade corporate default rate to fall slightly, to 3.75% by June 2025 from 4.8% in June 2024.

Looking ahead, I think the overall default rate may expect some improvement from the FED rate cut in September and potentially from the future rate cuts. I believe that the bulk of effects from the credit losses have been built into BDCs portfolio, because their loans have been marketed to market on a quarterly basis. More importantly, some of the key macro challenges have been stabilizing and even improving. The impacts from rate cycle change will be absorbed and I could expect a more friendly growth support for mid companies that BDCs typically target.

Other signals of BDC recovery can be seen from the "insiders" of the constituents of PBDC. Recently, there have been insider activities in purchasing company stocks. For example, the CEO of Golub Capital increased his stake by 10%. Two executives of Blue Owl Capital also purchased OBDC stocks in August. In my opinion, insider purchases often demonstrate strong internal confidence in the company's short-term prospects.

Active Managed portfolio is a great way to get involved with BDC investment

From a market perspective, prior to the recent correction, the trading prices of many BDCs had already reached or exceeded their book value. I believe that the pull back for many of the BDCs may see a bottom forming and in some cases ready to recover.

It will be difficult and time consuming for individual investors to dig in BDCs huge portfolio (loans up to several hundreds). The leverage level could also be very high and different for BDCs. For example, PBDC's top holding Ares Capital has a total leverage of 49.67%, while the other top constituent Blue Owl Capital leverages at 53.47%. So overall, I believe that the ETF will inject more diversification and defensive bias against certain risks inherited in BDCs.

On the other hand, the PBDC yield (9.65%) remains competitive. The total return has shown a strong performance thanks to it "actively" managed portfolio. PBDC is a good choice for the income investors who want to start BDC exposures now.

I have been recently increasing my PBDC positions gradually. I am expecting PBDC to continue to outperform VanEck BDC Income ETF(BIZD), which is the largest BDC ETF with $1.22B AUM and a total of 29 BDCs in its portfolio. BIZD is often viewed as a benchmark ETF. The following shows the performance comparison of the two ETFs for past two years.

Notice that PBDC has not only outperformed BIZD in total returns, but also has slightly edged up against the S&P 500 index since its inception

I have also opened a smaller position with BIZD for the portfolio nature and the double-digit yield (11.15%). Both ETFs are good income holdings for me and I think their price performance will be further improved over the time when the normal growth of US economy can be resumed.

Risks and Caveats

The recession is the worst enemy to BDCs. Even a mild recession could bring down BDC stocks badly. This is one of the main situations when the credit could be deteriorating rather rapidly. Keep in mind that BDCs invest in midsize private companies with high leverages. Investors should keep recessionary indicators on a high alert.

Due to these high yields and high leverages, BDCs often carry additional risks and can be quickly sold off. Although I suggest holding PBDC to offset volatility risks, this can only be achieved to a certain extent. The sector level drawdowns cannot really be mediated.

Conclusion

BDC market has seen some weakness in the past few months for the aftermath of the high-rate cycle, including issues such as "idiosyncratic losses" of credit and rating deteriorating. However these are not systemic issues and seem past behind us over time due to BDC quarterly mark-to-market and the resilient US economy. The BDCs remain an excellent income stream for investors looking for double-digit yields. PBDC as a portfolio of BDCs is a good start. I rate it a BUY and recommend income investor to put it on their radar screen.

data.title: CGMS: Diversified Bond ETF With High 5.8% Yield And Strong Performance
data.description:
The Capital Group U.S. Multi-Sector Income ETF (NYSEARCA:CGMS) is an actively managed, multi-sector bond ETF, focusing on corporate bonds, leaning investment-grade. It sports an above-average 5.8% dividend yield, has outperformed since inception due to savvy security selection and alpha, with lower realized volatility to boot. Considering elevated interest rates and the success of CGMS's investment strategy in the past, I rate the fund a buy.

CGMS - Overview and Analysis

Strategy and Portfolio

CGMS is an actively managed, multi-sector bond ETF. It is an incredibly well-diversified fund, with investments in over 500 issuers from several bond sub-asset classes, including corporate bonds, treasuries, CLOs and MBS, and with some exposure to international developed markets. It focuses heavily on investment-grade corporate bonds. Asset allocations are as follows:

CGMS is actively managed so asset allocations and broader investment decisions are active decisions taken by / dependent on the fund's management team. CGMS chose to focus on investment-grade bonds, attempting to increase income and returns, or decrease risk. Index funds, including the Vanguard Total Bond Market ETF (BND), simply track an index, aiming to achieve index (read market) returns.

CGMS's approach has higher potential returns, although risks are higher too, and returns more dependent on its management team. I'm quite bullish on CGSM's manager, the Capital Group, as it operates several best-in-class ETFs, including the Capital Group Dividend Value ETF (CGDV):

Credit Quality

CGMS leans to investment-grade securities, with significant investments in non-investment, and some investments in cash and unrated securities. It leans investment-grade, although not significantly so.

CGMS's credit quality should result in some losses during downturns and recessions, performing somewhere between investment-grade and high-yield ETFs. CGMS has yet to experience such a scenario, as it's a young fund, with inception in late 2023. The closest scenario was the mini drawdown of late July / early August, as negative economic news and the partial unwinding of the Yen carry trade wreaked havoc on the market. CGMS was slightly up during the drawdown, performing somewhere between investment-grade and non-investment grade bonds.

Overall, I would expect CGMS to perform somewhat similar to the above during more prolonged downturns and recessions. I think slight losses are likelier than gains, as credit spreads tend to skyrocket during recessions, while barely budging during the above.

Interest Rate Risk

CGMS leans short-term securities, with an average duration of 4.1 years. Most bonds and bond sub-asset classes have somewhat higher durations, with high-yield bonds being the sole major exception.

CGMS's below-average duration is almost certainly an active decision taken by its management team, as investment-grade corporate bonds, which comprise most of its portfolio, have a much higher duration of 8.5 years. Duration doesn't get cut in half due to coincidence or due to third factors, so a decision has to be it. Said decision seems to have been the wrong one, at least on a returns basis, with corporate bonds seeing declining rates since CGMS's inception, treasuries flat.

Overall Risk and Volatility

CGMS has some credit risk, below-average interest rate risk. The net effect seems somewhat unclear, although the fund could not plausibly be either significantly more or less volatile than average (too middle of the road for either). CGMS's realized volatility seems slightly below-average, broadly in-line with expectations.

Looking at the fund's strategy, portfolio, risk and volatility, it seems clear that this is a vanilla, middle of the road ETF. There are few significant risks here, or massive deviations from the benchmark. It's active management could play a decisive role here, which brings me to my next two points.

Dividend Yield Analysis

CGMS sports a 5.8% dividend yield, reasonably good on an absolute basis, and higher than that of most bonds. Same is true for SEC yields and yields to maturity, both more relevant metrics after significant interest rate changes.

CGMS's above-average dividend yield is quite likely an active investment decision: the fund chose to focus on higher-yield bonds to generate more in income and achieve higher returns. The strategy seems to be working, as evidenced by the fund's strong performance. Let's have a look.

Performance Track-Record

CGMS's performance track-record is quite strong, albeit short, with the fund seeing double-digit annual returns since inception. Absolute returns were driven by elevated interest rates / dividends, courtesy of past Federal Reserve hikes, and higher bond prices, as interest rates slowly decline. Do remember that interest rates declining might lead to short-term bond gains, but are a net negative long-term.

CGMS's relative returns are strong, too, with the fund outperforming most bonds and bond sub-asset classes since inception. Returns look particularly strong compared to broader bond indexes, as well as treasuries and short-term investment-grade corporate bonds. Returns are only marginally higher than of those investment-grade corporate bonds more broadly, and effectively identical to those of high-yield bonds (do remember these have much higher credit risk).

CGMS's returns seem easy to explain. The fund has outperformed most of its peers due to savvy security selection, as evidenced by its significant outperformance relative to short-term IG bonds, and as per its latest annual report. It's above-average dividend yield played a positive role too, although the spreads here are too tight to explain such a massive outperformance. CGMS's below-average duration was a net negative, as interest rates have declined since inception, and as evidenced by the iShares iBoxx $ Investment Grade Corporate Bond ETF (LQD) almost matching its performance (remember these have the highest duration). Seems like one bad decision almost cancelled out several good ones, although not quite.

CGSM's returns and outperformance are both somewhat consistent, with the fund performing quite well during most time periods (not too many of these though). As an example, fund returns YTD:

CGSM's overall track-record seems strong. Returns themselves are high, outperformance common, and both are somewhat consistent. Considering risks are somewhat below-average, risk-adjusted returns should be strong too. Both Sharpe and Sortino ratios are above-average, as expected. Biggest issue here is the fund's short track-record. A year or two of outperformance could be luck or volatility, a decades-long track-record almost certainly alpha. CGSM has only existed for a few years, so we really can't rule out luck or volatility for its results.

CGMS and Peers

I've covered several similar ETFs to CGMS in the past few months, including the Angel Oak Income ETF (CARY), the BlackRock Flexible Income ETF (BINC), and the JPMorgan Income ETF (JPIE). As these are quite similar choices, thought to do a quick comparison.

CGMS's 5.8% dividend yield is marginally lower than that of CARY and BINC, but the spreads here are not meaningful, and are partly due to normal ETF dividend volatility. BINC has the lowest 5.5% dividend yield.

CGMS has slightly higher credit risk, somewhat higher duration. Overall volatility is slightly higher too.

CGMS has outperformed its peers since inception, with no significant or long periods of underperformance.

CARY has the strongest risk-adjusted returns though, slightly underperforming CGMS at moderately decreased volatility. CARY's share price and returns are both more stable, as can be seen below.

Overall, CGMS is a higher-risk, higher-return investment than its peers, but only slightly so.

Conclusion

CGMS is an actively managed, multi-sector bond ETF, focusing on corporate bonds, leaning investment-grade. It sports an above-average 5.8% dividend yield, has seen strong, above-average returns since inception, with lower realized volatility. Elevated interest rates and the continued success of its investment strategy should lead to good income and returns moving forward, making the fund a buy.

data.title: Big Bitcoin Bull Breakout In Progress (Technical Analysis)
data.description:
As I have been anticipating in recent articles, the next big bull run in Bitcoin (BTC-USD) is now in progress. New all-time weekly and monthly closing highs are a stone's throw away, and once those levels have been cracked, the new bull will be in the open field. My targets for this move are $180k-$300k.

Some cite relatively high levels of "Greed" as measured by the Crypto Fear/Greed Index as a reason why BTC can't rally on from here.

While that can be the case under certain conditions, if we look back at the most analogous setup in the prior history of the Bitcoin market, we can see that the current situation of the peak of March 2021 and the breakout happening now is analogous to the period between the peak of December 2017 and the big breakout of September 2020.

If we overlay the Fear/Greed Index on the monthly chart, we find that the "Greed" level of today matches that which existed just as the 2020 rally was getting underway. It's worth noting that monthly RSI was also at the same level as today, back in 2020.

The structures and durations of both setups are remarkably similar, so it is not an out of distribution possibility that the results could be similar. Greed could reach maximum levels and stay there for quite some time as Bitcoin rallies hard. In the prior instance, that resulted in a 429% rally from the breakout point.

This situation is very similar in many respects to sentiment indicators such as the Bull/Bear ratio starting a bull run from a relatively high level, or technical indicators being already "overbought". Bears will often point out that a bull "can't" begin under such conditions...but this often turns out to be incorrect. Bullishness and "overbought" levels can persist for quite some time as a bull move continues unabated.

In this particular case, however, the technical condition of the Bitcoin market has plenty of upside wiggle room. The weekly chart, for example:

Here we can see that price is breaking out above the upper rail of the red flag consolidation pattern. Weekly RSI has lots of upside before getting into its resistance zone. The lengthy consolidation in the area of the prior all-time highs is extremely bullish.

Here's the daily:

Breaking out above the pattern. Daily RSI will reach resistance fast, but it can stay up there for a while, or even correct a bit, as the market continues to advance.

Short term:

Breaking out. Could get a little sticky in the 72-74k area as the last of the bears sell out. But after that, there's no more resistance, and the bull has a clear field to charge ahead.

No technical setup is completely unambiguous or a "slam dunk", but this one is among the stronger bull cases I have seen in almost 20 years of analyzing markets.

data.title: Ford: The Pessimism Has Gone Too Far
data.description:
Ford: Underperforming Peers And Market

Ford Motor Company (NYSE:F) investors have had a highly challenging year, underperforming the S&P 500 (SPX) (SPY) and its cross-town arch-rival, General Motors (GM). Therefore, the market seems to have delivered its verdict on CEO Jim Farley and his team (at least for 2024).

In my previous bullish Ford article in May 2024, I argued that Ford's increasingly profitable commercial business should help bolster its core business, even as the legacy automaker transitions to EVs.

However, Ford's EV growth roadmap has faced unanticipated stumbles, as the market likely assessed F as falling behind General Motors in its pursuit. Although Ford's core internal combustion engine business has continued to underpin its sector-leading profitability ("A+" profitability grade), the market seems unconvinced. Although F's attractive forward dividend yields should attract income investors to stay vested, growth investors have likely turned their attention elsewhere, given GM's more robust "C+" growth grade, well above F's disappointing "D-" growth grade.

Ford: Must Prove Its Path Toward Profitable EV Scaling

As someone who never likes to bet against the market, Ford investors must assess whether the market pessimism is reasonable, as automotive companies have undergone challenging growth deceleration over the past year. EV market leader Tesla (TSLA) has faced challenges reigniting its growth profile, likely worsened by its sky-high valuation metrics. Notwithstanding its expensive valuation, TSLA investors have outperformed Ford investors since April, as investors likely placed a higher emphasis on its growth recovery and autonomous driving and robotaxi ambitions.

Ford's ability to compete more effectively in EVs will likely be crucial in lifting the market's optimism through 2025. Ford will release its third-quarter earnings on October 28, affording investors an update on its business outlook. As a reminder, Ford is expected to ramp up its hybrid strategy, as the growth in pure-play BEVs has slowed markedly.

Ford reported a 0.7% increase in Q3 US sales, corroborating the market's caution. Q3's growth was significantly lower than the 2.7% average Ford notched YTD through September 2024. While the growth profile in hybrids (up 38% YoY in Q2) surpassed the growth rate (up 12% YoY in Q3) of BEVs, investors are likely increasingly concerned whether Ford's declining ICE business can help sustain its EV transition.

Ford: Growth Environment Has Turned More Competitive

As seen above, Ford has suffered a significant revenue growth deceleration since its peak in 2022. Furthermore, analysts expect Ford's sales to slow down through 2025, raising the execution risks in an increasingly challenging EV growth environment. Notwithstanding the EV transition risks, Ford's core ICE business is still profitable, critical to helping to sustain stable automotive adjusted EBIT margins through FY2026.

As seen above, Ford Blue (ICE segment) is still expected to post an adjusted EBIT of between $6B and $6.5B. However, it was revised downward from its prior outlook of between $7B and $7.5B. However, the strength of its commercial business (Ford Pro) is expected to mitigate the weakness of its EV business and the slowdown of its core ICE business. As a reminder, Ford Pro's adjusted EBIT outlook was revised upward in Q2, corroborating its growth momentum.

General Motors has also unveiled its mass-market EV model, demonstrating its resolve to compete more aggressively. GM's trajectory is notable, as the leading automakers have suffered several setbacks in China. Chinese EVs, led by BYD Company's (OTCPK:BYDDY) (OTCPK:BYDDF) incredible rise, have dented foreign automakers' market leadership as they struggle to transition effectively into EVs.

Notwithstanding my caution, Ford's downgraded Wall Street earnings estimates suggest a possible growth inflection point from FY2026. In addition, Ford's ICE business is still anticipated to be a core profitability driver, bolstering the confidence of income investors. Hence, it should help underpin F's relative undervaluation, suggesting much pessimism has likely been reflected.

While F's valuation grades have remained relatively undervalued compared to its consumer discretionary sector (XLY) peers, it has still underperformed. It's a critical reminder for investors not to focus solely on its valuation when assessing F's risk/reward profile.

As a reminder, F's adjusted EPS multiple of just below 6x is more than 60% below its sector median. However, it's markedly above its automotive peers' median of 3.6x. Therefore, Ford still needs to justify improved growth prospects relative to its sector peers. Despite that, its relatively low adjusted EPS multiple should also provide a valuation floor for the stock as the company attempts a more profitable transition for its EV business. In addition, its relatively low free cash flow multiple of just under 4x is more than 60% below its sector median. As a result, I assess that the market seems to have baked in significant pessimism, even as the company's ICE and Ford Pro business are still crucial profit drivers.

Ford's EV strategy has come under intense scrutiny as it faces an increasingly competitive EV market. The Chinese EV leaders are expected to up the ante in their quest for global domination. Therefore, Ford must justify its ability to demonstrate a clear path toward EV profitability. Thus, GM's ability to scale faster could impact the market's confidence in F, potentially affecting its ability to recover from its underperformance.

Despite that, the market sentiments toward automakers could improve through 2025, as the Fed is expected to turn increasingly dovish. With interest rate headwinds expected to have peaked in 2024, investors will likely bank on improved buying sentiments in the automotive business as the drag on large purchase consumer spending becomes less of a drag. Hence, it should also underpin F's relative undervaluation as the market assesses a potential valuation re-rating to levels more in line with its sector peers.

Is F Stock A Buy, Sell, Or Hold?

F's price chart indicates the stock has been mired in an extended consolidation zone above the $9 support level, while buying advances have struggled above the $15 level.

Therefore, I observed robust dip-buying support at F's October 2023 and August 2024 lows as investors loaded up. The stock's attractive forward yields should help underpin its appeal, as the Fed is expected to lower interest rates through 2025. Therefore, I assess its $9 support zone should remain constructive, allowing more aggressive opportunities to add exposure.

F's recent recovery from its August 2024 lows has remained resilient, notwithstanding its relative market underperformance. I assess that the market is likely looking for more proof points in Ford's EV growth recovery before lifting its battered valuation further. Despite that, its risk/reward still looks attractive, although investors must be cautious about expecting market performance through 2025.

Rating: Maintain Buy (under review).

Important note: Investors are reminded to do their due diligence and not rely on the information provided as financial advice. Consider this article as supplementing your required research. Please always apply independent thinking. Note that the rating is not intended to time a specific entry/exit at the point of writing, unless otherwise specified.

I Want To Hear From You

Have constructive commentary to improve our thesis? Spotted a critical gap in our view? Saw something important that we didn’t? Agree or disagree? Comment below with the aim of helping everyone in the community to learn better!

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Rate Cuts To Boost OneMain Financial
data.description:
OneMain Holdings (NYSE:OMF) is a stock I've covered twice before. The first article was my inaugural piece for Seeking Alpha, in which I went over the business model and why OMF was a leader and subprime lending. I closed that Buy thesis with:

While potential always exists for greater stress to hit their loan book than they have anticipated, OneMain Financial stands out as a deft reformer among subprime lenders in the years that followed the associated financial crisis. Today's buyers should be conservative and not expect quick returns. OMF will provide long-term value primarily through its dividend and modest capital appreciation.

In my second article, I maintain my Buy rating, noting OMF's sturdiness in a period of heightened rates and its ability to engage in M&A during these times:

While no materially major changes occurred in Q4, we did finally get the FY 2023 results, and (crucially) we finally saw the seeds of OneMain's future being planted: auto-finance and credit cards. Leveraging the expertise from their core business in personal loans, OneMain will be a position to grow its high-yield loan book as it enters these markets on favorable terms.

With challenges in the year so far and Q3 results on the horizon, I thought another update was in order. I think the year's progress speaks to OneMain's resilience, and I remain confident that it is a Buy as we approach greener pastures.

YTD 2024 Results

As I did not follow up after the first couple of quarters, let's take a look at the YTD results for 2024.

First thing to note is that net finance receivables continued to grow, in spite of the distress previously discussed by the company.

Much of this owes to the acquisition of Foursight Capital, which closed at the start of Q2. This acquisition allows OneMain to take their underwriting strengths and connect it to Foursight's auto-dealer networks.

Some of it can also be attributed to their other new product: the BrightWay credit cards. With the testing period done, OneMain is steadily rolling it out to new customers.

Delinquency rates have also been lower, after peaking in Q4 2023, suggesting the worst of the credit stress caused by the Fed's rate hikes and their cooling of the economy has passed.

Yet, this has not been only a matter of OneMain idly rolling with interest rates. It's a story of active portfolio management as well. As rate hikes began, the company began a credit tightening strategy, to write loans that had the risk parameters to endure a tougher macro-environment. These newer loans (the "front book") have contributed to the lower delinquencies as the older ones run off.

As the earnings are concerned, they remain positive but lower compared to years prior. As net interest income and insurance revenues are up, the main reason is the aforementioned delinquencies.

In spite of this, OneMain continues to feel comfortable about the health of its balance sheet, with buybacks and dividends continuing. They even increased their quarterly dividend by 4% in Q2.

Future Outlook

As I remain optimistic about the company, I'll lay out the near-term and long-term factors, as well as some of the risks that still exist as we enter a period of rate cuts.

Near-Term

Rate cuts will be the biggest influence in the near future. Speaking during the Q2 2024 earnings call, CEO Doug Shulman explained what generally occurs in a hypothetical where rates go down (reminder that this was before cuts were announced in September):

Consumer lending is a cyclical business. And you can see a world where the consumer is feeling a lot better. Interest rates are coming down; credits getting much better and a bunch of things could be moving in the right direction. We feel pretty good that even in a tricky environment, our metrics are now both moving in the right direction where we've seen a modest uptick in origination, and we see the credit trends moving in the right direction.

A month into our first cut and with another FOMC meeting coming in November, there's reason to think that OMF's portfolio will start to fare better.

Another reassuring sign is that real wage growth grew as inflation shrank and fell below the nominal wage growth. As this trend has persisted throughout 2024, I believe more borrowers one OneMain's book should be in a better position to pay in full. It also speaks well for newer originations going forward.

At the September Barclays investor conference, Shulman spoke to this as well, stating that the general consumer has "stabilized," even if they remain "skittish" at these nominally higher prices.

Long-Term

Reiterating somewhat from my original thesis, the real value of OneMain is in its steady value accumulation over time, not rapid growth. Shulman spoke to this once more in the earnings call:

I think, in terms of growth, we are being deliberate. We are not chasing growth across any of our products right now. We have very tight credit boxes across personal loans, auto and card. But as I've talked about before, Foursight gives us now a platform that as we scale this business deliberately, we'll be able to build it over time. I imagine the significant growth will happen once we feel that some of the macro uncertainty is cleared and things have turned fully.

This is evidenced by the steady stone-setting OneMain has undertaken during their period of credit tightening. They used the context of high inflation and rate hikes as a testing ground for their BrightWay product. They engaged in M&A while other lenders have been trying to salvage their balance sheets. These things don't produce growth in the year that they occur. They keep the business in the game so that they can consolidate their advantages.

Moreover, as the recently increased dividend yields in the high, single digits, a significant portion of the return will be these distributions over the lifetime of the business.

Valuation

My previous valuation used a method similar to Discounted Cash Flow and substituted free cash flow with conservative estimate of EPS.

Using earnings figures at the time as a guide (GAAP earnings and capital generation), I came up with $6.25 per share. From there I surmised that 3% EPS growth was an achievable over time as they steadily grow their balance sheet, repurchase shares, and expand with M&A. 3%, along with my terminal multiple of 5, were designed to make it harder to overvalue this business.

Above, I made only a slight change, reducing my baseline EPS to $6.00, in light of losses from delinquent loans this year. That reduces my calculation of intrinsic value from about $60.50 to $58. It's a minor difference but one I wanted to get down.

Additionally, I'll remind folks that both are priced for a 10% discount rate (typical return of the market). As it stands, OMF remains below this $58 price.

Risks

Even with my optimism and efforts to avoid overvaluation, let's consider some more risks.

Referencing the skittishness consumers have faced, even as real wages have managed to catch up with inflation, this could indicate that the growth they intended after the cyclical swing in rates may not occur as quickly as they wish. Customers may spend more conservatively for longer than expected.

Another risk is regulatory. As these loans are issued at APRs well over 20%, many view OneMain's business as usury. Political pressure at the state or federal level could result in regulatory crackdowns that prevent OneMain from pricing their loans for the risk profiles of their customers.

Lastly, with the acquisition of Foursight, OneMain's results will now follow more the cyclical trends in the auto industry, and problems there could, to a greater degree than before, be a problem for OneMain.

Q3 Outlook

All these things considered, I don't expect many major changes to occur for Q3's results, which will be out on Oct. 30. I think the important details will be qualitative. How are their customers are feeling? How are they responding to the first rate cut? Will there be anything to worth reporting about the integration of Foursight into their auto line?

These are examples of the questions I ask myself as the earnings day approaches.

Conclusion

OneMain Financial continues to be the preeminent lender of subprime customers. 2024 has been a tough year so far, but the company has used this time well to prepare itself for the turn in the cycle, while not over-extending their balance sheet. Because of their religious risk aversion and attractive discount to its market price, I still consider it a Buy at these prices.

data.title: Leggett & Platt: Not Yet Low Enough
data.description:
Dear readers/followers,

It's been some years since I last covered Leggett & Platt (NYSE:LEG). In my last article, which you'll have to go back around 3 years to find, I went to a "Hold" for this company and sold my position about 2 months after the fact as the fundamentals worsened. You can find the last article on the company here.

The result since that rating is the following, which means that I view it as the "right" sort of rating.

This is a non-trivial decline for this sort of company. We're essentially talking about a 110% underperformance that I avoided, and others avoided, by not investing in this company. This is no small feat, especially since at the time the company was actually a fairly high-yield dividend stock and one that was fairly popular by my estimate.

In this article, I'm going to provide a long-needed update on what has happened to Leggett & Platt and where we could see this company going from here.

My minimum rate of return, conservatively, that I am looking for is around 15% per year. This is something that we're looking at if I'm able to get here, and what the company has been doing for the past few years.

Let's see what Leggett & Platt has done here.

Leggett & Platt - The company's downturn is based on some fairly clear trends, and they're unlikely to significantly improve in the near term

Leggett & Platt is a 141-year-old company. For a company like this to take the sort of nosedive that we've been seeing, it's quite a few things that have to happen.

Leggett & Platt is a business that, when I wrote about it last and as I am writing about it now, focuses on the manufacturing, development, and sales of bedding products, furniture, flooring, textiles, and the like. It's working with 15 different business units, organized into three segments.

These engineered components and products are not "bad" per se. And looking at the company that manufactures them and its traditions, it's not necessarily bad either. But the last years since the pandemic has not been good for the company. It's not just competition either, from an international basis, but inflation and other trends as well.

The recent results we have for Leggett & Platt confirm the continued downturn and decline for the company. 2Q24 sales were at $1.1B, which is another increase of 8% YoY, with both a volume and a raw material price decline. EBIT was down as well, in this case over $20M since the last quarter, and the company's adjusted EBIT margin is down 1.2% - which is significant given that the company's EBIT margin is less than 7.5%, to begin with, and now down to 6.3%. EPS is down more than 10% as well, and the company has updated its sales guidance and EPS estimate.

Leggett & Platt is now expecting sales to go no higher than $4.5B, versus a prior expected level of $4.65, and an overall adjusted EPS of no higher than $1.25, but more likely around $1.1/share, according to my own forecasts.

Highlights and positives for the company were fairly slim. Every single KPI that matters was negative for the quarter. EBITDA was down almost 25%, and this is from an already fairly low level to begin with. One of the few positives was a very slight decrease in working capital QoQ and YoY, but 30 bps is so marginal that I wouldn't consider it to matter all that much here, to be honest.

Net debt, despite the company changing things up, is actually increasing due to lower EBITDA. We're now at 3.83x net debt/R12 adjusted EBITDA - again, yet another negative for the company.

So what really remains for Leggett & Platt here? We have a BBB- credit rating, we have over 64% long-term debt/capital despite significant dividend cuts, and we're down to a rate of market capitalization that doesn't even cover 30% of the annual sales, implying a high lack of confidence for this company as things stand. The yield, which was once at over 5%, is now down to 1.47% even with the cut dividend rate.

The risks I see to Leggett & Platt here are numerous - and they go beyond just the fact that most of this sort of manufacturing for Home Furnishing is now done in other geographies outside of the US, where LEG currently manufactures. It also has to do with cost inflation, asset age, with the company's sales trends, which are down. We can also point to automotive exposure, which is significant here, and overall exposure to the industrial macro, which again - exists. Furniture and bedding companies that focus on quality and local production can make things work - this has been proven by local manufacturers across the world, but in order for Leggett & Platt to make this work, they need to change up further.

So what are the company's plans for this? And above all, what is the company telling us that it's going to do?

Leggett & Platt points to things like its strong competitive local position with very few large competitors, and large addressable markets inside and out of the US. It's fairly historically focused, arguing for its history of cash generation, a solid balance sheet with long-term sub-2x Net debt targets, and again, its management history, knowledge, and understanding of the overall business.

LEG's restructuring plan is in full force, and operational efficiencies according to the company are on track. It's unfortunate, then, that these aren't more visible at this time. Though, to be fair, the company also says that it's "strategic review" is currently underway as well.

I've re-reviewed LEG now in terms of exposures, and what I expect. What worries me is the 45% exposure to Automotive and Industrial macro (these are not my numbers, these are the company's own numbers), with the remaining being consumer durables - although durables might be pushing it, given the competitive position that the company is in here.

The company's dividend cut and current other ambitions all have a number of goals. These include things like maintaining IG, (although they recently increased the RCF to allow for a 4x leverage ratio, meaning a $1.2B revolver is now there), and plans to repay some notes in November.

So how is this increase expected to be delivered during these next few years? Fairly simple - the restructuring plan, meeting the operational efficiency targets, and expected demand improvements - some of which are outside of the company's control. What is also beyond the company's influence is cost recovery of raw materials and pricing - given that pricing is also an external factor, and if other players change their pricing, this company might have to do so as well.

The aforementioned restructuring plan Is showing some promise - it would be unfair to say otherwise (shifting to some positives here). The company has moved all of its spring production to the locations that remain, completed its China innerspring downsizing, and closed a number of plants, with more consolidations coming. Also, several assets are currently on sale or sold, with another several planned. In furniture, the company has restructured as well, closing plants, moving the engineering team, and once again, selling/selling properties.

All In all, the company is moving forward with its plan, but what will remain after this plan is finished remains to be seen - all we have now are estimates, and here are the overall estimated impacts at this time.

The core question becomes, do we want to invest in a home furniture/bedding/industrial turnaround at this valuation and with this upside? Because, one positive I will be clear about here is the fact that we are actually at an extremely low valuation, compared to where the company was historically.

Leggett & Platt - Upside to a conservative growth rate, but with impaired fundamentals and issues

My last article on this company was 4 years ago. I view this company as an investment I managed to avoid, but one I was never close in pushing into at the time given what else was available.

The trend for the company since has been abysmal, a word I don't use lightly, but which is warranted here given what you can see here in terms of trends.

Going forward, it seems clear to me that at some point we might see a recovery - but even if we go by the rates of recovery forecasted here, we're not necessarily seeing a very impressive sort of overall trend for the next 2-3 years. At a conservative 12-13x P/E, the RoR is below 15% annualized - and that's inclusive of the meager yield the company offers here.

The simple fact is, I don't see the valuation-related upside here that I would be interested in seeing if I were to take the risk - and it is a risk - of investing in Leggett & Platt.

I do not believe the company is going bankrupt, and I do believe that there is value here in the common equity; ergo, there is a valuation where I would "Buy" this company. But in this sort of situation, I tend to look for 25-30% annualized RoR - and those are conservative, not optimistic. What I mean is that I would want 25-30% annualized based on a low implied P/E multiple, not something that's outside of the company's justified trading range.

I do not have that here - we do not have that here. What we have is 6-12% at 10-12x P/E, and around 15-17% annualized at 14-15x P/E - and that's far from enough for me.

I would be interested to wait and see what happens when 3Q and 4Q come out here - because I expect 3Q, which is coming in a relatively short while (hence the preview here) to confirm the continued downward trajectory in the face of not exactly improving macro. I believe it will take until early or mid-2025 until we see definite signs of a turnaround, maybe even longer - and that means I believe we have time until we need to decide whether to invest here.

Average analyst targets confirm my stance. I would give the company something of $15/share if things turn around, which means that we want a higher upside. But the 3 analysts following the company here go from $11 - $14/share, with an average of $12.5/share, and 3 out of them are at a "Hold" recommendation here. That means none of them believes it is time "yet".

I agree with this assessment, and give the company a rating in accordance with this, as seen below, for October 2024.

Thesis

  • Leggett & Platt could be a company you want to buy during a turnaround, provided that you do so at the right price. I do still like the company management, the way the company seems to handle its business, and the company's market-leading position as well as its very disciplined use of cash. Some of the issues the company has faced here is certainly outside of its sphere of influence, and this should be noted.
  • However, all that withstanding, the company is not exactly an outperformer, nor even an expected outperformer in the near term. While growth is expected to increase from here on out, it's not expected to increase in 2024 - and in fact, the company has recently negatively updated its guidance for the year.
  • Given the lack of visibility, the overall higher correlation to an unfavorable macro, including automotive, industrial, and furniture, a sub-par yield, BBB- credit, and its recent history, I rate Leggett & Platt as a "Hold" and add my voice to the choir of investors who are now on the sidelines.

Here are my criteria and how the company fulfills them (italicized).

  • This company is overall qualitative.
  • This company is fundamentally safe/conservative & well-run.
  • This company pays a well-covered dividend.
  • This company is currently cheap.
  • This company has a realistic upside based on earnings growth or multiple expansion/reversion.

Quality is a question following the company's sub-par development. Its dividend is cut, but well-covered here - and it's not cheap. Its upside is speculative here, and I think it's better to wait. 2/5, and a "Hold".
